---
title: "Tier 03 — Learn"
---

```{python}
# | echo: false
import jammi_cookbook
```

**Recipe:** `fine_tune_graph` · **Theory:** representation learning on graphs —
node2vec / contrastive (Stanković et al. 2020, Part III; Hamilton 2017) · **Rail:**
measurement (declared-edge gain).

Tier 02 *propagated* fixed embeddings; tier 03 **learns** new ones. `fine_tune_graph`
samples random walks over the declared citation graph and fine-tunes the encoder
contrastively: papers that co-occur on a walk are pulled together, others pushed
apart. The graph supervises the metric.

The keystone ran this on the GPU server with `edge_provenance="declared"`:

```python
db.fine_tune_graph(
    node_source=papers, id_column="paper_id", text_column="abstract",
    edge_source=cite_edges, src_column="src", dst_column="dst",
    base_model="answerdotai/ModernBERT-base", edge_provenance="declared",
    epochs=1,
)
```

and committed the resulting checkpoint id. The requested `epochs` is honored
exactly by the engine. This chapter reads the cache on CPU.

```{python}
from jammi_cookbook import contracts, rails

ft = contracts.load_artifact("arxiv.ft_model")
print("fine-tuned model id:", ft["model_id"])
print("edge provenance:    ", ft["edge_provenance"])
print("base model:         ", ft["base_model"])
```

## The measurement rail: did the declared graph help?

The honest test of graph supervision is whether the fine-tuned encoder retrieves
same-subject papers better than the base encoder. The keystone measured recall\@10
for the declared-edge fine-tune and froze it; the gain over the tier-01 base is the
learned signal.

```{python}
base_recall = contracts.golden("arxiv.tier01.recall_at_10").value
ft_recall = contracts.golden("arxiv.tier03.recall_at_10").value
gain = contracts.golden("arxiv.tier03.recall_gain_vs_base").value

print(f"recall@10 base encoder:        {base_recall:.3f}")
print(f"recall@10 declared-edge FT:    {ft_recall:.3f}")
print(f"Δ (graph-supervised gain):     {gain:+.3f}")
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
rails.measure("arxiv.tier03.recall_at_10", ft_recall)
rails.measure("arxiv.tier03.recall_gain_vs_base", gain)
assert abs((ft_recall - base_recall) - gain) < 1e-6
```

## The circularity contract

Why `edge_provenance="declared"` and not `"similarity"`? Fine-tuning on a
similarity graph trains the encoder to reproduce the metric it was *already* using
— a circular objective that learns nothing new. The **declared** citation graph
carries who-cited-whom, a signal the embeddings cannot reconstruct, so it can
actually move the metric. This is the circularity contract: only a *declared*
graph supervises a non-trivial gain. The keystone's positive declared-edge gain
is that contract demonstrated, not asserted.

## Bridge note (a signature chapter — deepened in the bridge chapters)

> **Graph-supervised metric learning = contrastive fine-tune over walk-sampled
> declared-edge pairs.** node2vec / DeepWalk's skip-gram over random walks (Grover
> & Leskovec 2016; the monograph's Part III graph embeddings; Hamilton et al.
> 2017) ↔ `fine_tune_graph(edge_provenance="declared")`. The walk sampler turns
> the citation graph into positive pairs; the contrastive loss is the learning
> objective; `edge_provenance` is the circularity guard.
